event_name: UFC Fight Night: Song vs. Figueiredo

fights.fighter_a_bio: Song Yadong pressures forward with heavy hands and leg kicks, hunting knockdowns from the opening bell. He's chinny when backing up and eats counters on the way in—a big reason he's landed on his back in losses.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Figueiredo is a slick grappler who chains takedowns into ground control, then lands elbows from top position to slow his opponents down. The model heavily favors him (80.2%) because Song tends to get overwhelmed when dragged into scrambles.
fights.watch_for: Can Song stay composed in the clinch and avoid getting dumped? If Figueiredo locks a Thai plumb and yanks him down, it's over—Song's wrestling defense falls apart under pressure. Song needs to keep it at range and land early, before getting trapped.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Zhang Mingyang is a heavy-handed striker who hunts for clean shots and relies on precision over volume—his Elo rating of 1531.5 lags behind Menifield's, suggesting he hasn't faced top-tier opposition yet. Our model gives him a 92.6% win probability, mainly banking on his striking advantage against an opponent who hasn't proven elite hands.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Alonzo Menifield has a higher Elo (1582.8) because he's fought proven guys and has the grappling chops to drag strikers into ugly positions. At +188, he's the underdog, but he can neutralize Zhang's stand-up game if he commits to closing distance and controlling the clinch early.
fights.watch_for: Whether Menifield can survive Zhang's first two rounds of accurate striking long enough to wear him down and force a clinch—if Zhang lands clean and stays patient on the feet, Menifield's path to victory gets much smaller.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Pavlovich is a heavyweight anvil who absorbs punishment and counters with brutal knees and elbows in the clinch—he doesn't need perfect technique, just suffocating pressure and timing. Vegas and our model both see him as a heavy favorite (65%) because his 1586 Elo rating reflects a fighter who's beaten better opposition.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Teixeira hunts with kicks and combinations from distance, trying to pick apart opponents before they close the gap. At 1504 Elo, he's the cleaner striker here, but our model gives him only 35% odds—he'll need to stay mobile or risk getting dragged into Pavlovich's grind.
fights.watch_for: Can Teixeira keep the fight at range and pick Pavlovich apart with leg kicks and footwork, or does Pavlovich close the distance and chain takedowns into short elbows?

fights.fighter_a_bio: Kai Asakura pressures hard and mixes striking with clinch work to wear opponents down, with solid wrestling when he needs to reset. Vegas and our model both favor him heavily (-307, 65.7%), largely because his constant forward pressure and ability to land in combinations have proven effective at this level.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Smotherman has the wrestling pedigree to control exchanges and land ground-and-pound, but he doesn't force the action the way Asakura does. His defensive wrestling is solid, but he'll need to create openings rather than dictate—a tougher ask as a +246 underdog.
fights.watch_for: If Smotherman can get Asakura down early and keep him there, he buys time to impose his pace. If Asakura survives the first two rounds staying on his feet, his pressure advantage compounds and the fight tilts decisively in his favor.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Salikhov is a slick striker who lives in the pocket with combos and footwork, constantly angling in and out to land clean shots before backing off. He's dangerous when he can control distance and frustrate opponents with his movement, but gets beatable when forced into exchanges where grappling threat matters.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Matthews uses his frame and clinch game to smother opponents against the cage, grinding out control and looking for takedowns or short knees rather than trading in open space. He relies on suffocation tactics and positioning, which means he struggles badly when opponents keep him honest with kicks and distance management.
fights.watch_for: Can Matthews drag this into the clinch and cage time early, or will Salikhov's footwork and distance control keep the fight at range where his striking toolbox wins?

fights.fighter_a_bio: Perez pressures with volume striking while hunting takedowns—he doesn't let opponents set pace. Vegas and our model both favor him (53.6%) because his Elo rating shows he's faced and beaten tougher competition than Sumudaerji.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Sumudaerji counters best when he can create space and land heavy shots on the feet; he struggles when pinned against the cage. At +114, he's the underdog despite similar unpredictability, suggesting Vegas sees his grappling defense as the real vulnerability here.
fights.watch_for: Whether Perez can land takedowns early and control positioning—if Sumudaerji survives the first two rounds upright and trading, his counter-striking gives him a real path to the upset.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Yi Sak Lee enters with equal odds despite Vegas slightly backing Dias—our model gives him the edge here. Without detailed fight footage available, he's a true unknown quantity, which can mean opportunity if he's been developing weapons his record doesn't fully show.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Luis Felipe Dias is Vegas's pick (−172 favorite), suggesting consistent performances that betting markets trust more than our model does. He'll need to execute whatever his primary gameplan is cleanly, since the model sees real vulnerability in this matchup.
fights.watch_for: Whether Yi Sak Lee can disrupt Dias's rhythm early—if he lands something forcing Dias to adjust or respect a specific weapon in rounds 1–2, the underdog chaos index of 0.5 suggests this becomes a genuinely competitive fight.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Ding Meng is operating at an even skill baseline with no established fight tape to predict tendencies. Our model gives him a 50-50 shot, meaning this fight hinges entirely on execution and adjustments mid-fight.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Jose Henrique is similarly unproven at this level with identical win odds and Elo rating. Expect a true coin flip where fresh legs and early aggression could swing momentum either way.
fights.watch_for: Whoever controls the clinch and dictates pace in rounds 1-2 will likely steal the decision—both fighters are evenly matched, so ring generalship early becomes the tiebreaker.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Aoriqileng pressures forward with leg kicks and clinch work, grinding opponents into the fence rather than hunting finishes. Our model likes his heavy top control and pace—giving him a 93% edge—but his lower Elo (1503.9) suggests he's less proven than his competition record looks.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Haddon fights off his back foot, using distance and timing to land counters rather than initiating exchanges. Vegas has him favored at -359, but that conflicts with the Elo ratings and our model's assessment—a sharp split that says the oddsmakers may be betting name value over fight film.
fights.watch_for: If Haddon lets Aoriqileng pin him against the fence for three rounds, it's a wrestling-based decision win. If Haddon stays mobile and lands clean counters early, he punches above his odds.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Rei Tsuruya is a heavy grappler who drags opponents to the mat and controls them from top position—he's hardest to beat when the fight stays on the ground. Vegas and our model both like him here (52.6%), mainly because Aguilar will have to stuff takedowns and stay upright to win.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Jesus Aguilar mixes striking with takedown defense, but his Elo rating (1514.5) is actually higher than Tsuruya's despite being the underdog—Vegas is betting on Tsuruya's grappling advantage, not overall skill. He needs to land clean and avoid the clinch where Tsuruya hunts the takedown.
fights.watch_for: Does Aguilar keep the fight standing and make Tsuruya miss in the first two rounds, or does Tsuruya get him down early and smother him from top control? That's the whole fight.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Angela Hill pressures constantly with volume striking—she throws in bunches and forces opponents to react rather than lead. She's vulnerable to clinch work and takedowns if opponents can close distance without eating shots.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Xiong Jingnan is a submission threat with dominant grappling who controls fights from top position once she gets you down. She struggles when opponents keep the fight at range and avoid the clinch.
fights.watch_for: Whether Hill can maintain distance and land her striking combinations before Xiong closes the gap and drags her into the clinch where grappling becomes the game.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Zhu Kangjie comes in as a relative unknown with limited fight history, sitting at an even 1500 Elo rating that puts him on par with his opponent. Our model gives him a razor-thin 50.1% edge, suggesting this is a true toss-up with no clear technical advantage.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Ramon Taveras is equally unproven at 1499.5 Elo, making this a matchup between two fighters without much tape to separate them. The 0.1% difference in win probability shows this fight could realistically go either way.
fights.watch_for: Whoever controls the center of the octagon in rounds 1–2 will likely dictate the pace; expect the fighter who lands first and forces their opponent to react to win this unpredictable 50-50 fight.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Lookboonmee lands hard kicks and knees from the clinch, but relies heavily on that attack—when opponents stuff the clinch or keep distance, she doesn't have a reliable Plan B. She's the Vegas favorite here, but her limited striking volume and submission threat leave her vulnerable to someone who can control the pace.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Amorim pressures with body shots and leg kicks, then hunts takedowns when openings appear—she wins by accumulation and dictating where the fight happens. Our model gives her 92.3% to win, and Vegas roughly agrees; she's the far more complete striker with better cardio to maintain that pressure.
fights.watch_for: If Amorim lets Lookboonmee establish the clinch and trade knees early, that's Loma's best window—but if Amorim uses footwork to circle out and reset on the feet, she'll rack up damage and eventually take over the fight.
